docs.title: Home
docs.text:
Simcha Lazarus

Technical Writer

I’m a technical writer focused on turning complex systems into clear, usable documentation. Over the past couple of years, I’ve worked on both customer-facing and internal docs for SaaS products, including cybersecurity, API security, e-commerce, and mobile gaming.

Before that, I spent over 10 years writing content for high-tech companies, so I’m comfortable jumping between different audiences and levels of technical detail. I also work closely with product and engineering teams to make sure the docs reflect how the product actually behaves, not just how it’s supposed to.

I’m looking for a full-time role where I can keep improving how documentation is structured, written, and used—especially as products grow.

Outside of work, I like working on hands-on technical projects. Recently I’ve been learning electronics and repurposing old devices, and some of those projects are included here.

Writing Samples Projects Contact Me

docs.title: About
docs.text: I'm a Technical Writer with over 3 years of experience creating customer-facing and internal documentation for SaaS products. I've worked as a contractor across industries including cybersecurity, app and API security, e-commerce, and mobile gaming — and that variety has made me a quick learner and adaptable writer. I also have experience with UX writing and microcopy, and I'm used to collaborating closely with product, engineering, and customer-facing teams. I'm now looking for a full-time role where I can be a real part of a team, contribute to something lasting, and grow alongside the product.

docs.title: What I do
docs.text:
I specialise in developer-facing documentation: API references, integration guides, onboarding tutorials, and release notes. I work in a docs-as-code workflow — Markdown in Git, pull requests for review, CI/CD for deployment. I'm comfortable reading diffs, filing bugs, and pairing with engineers to nail down the behaviour that matters.

Outside of professional work, I document my own technical projects — the Projects section of this site. Writing while doing keeps my process honest and produces docs that reflect a real procedure, not an idealised one.

docs.title: Experience
docs.text:
Senior Technical Writer — Fintech SaaS, remote (2021 – present) Led documentation for three new API products. Built and maintained the internal style guide. Reduced support ticket volume by 30% through improved onboarding docs.

Technical Writer II — Cloud infrastructure company (2019 – 2021) Owned CLI reference docs and getting-started guides. Introduced the Diátaxis documentation framework across the docs team.

Technical Writer — SaaS analytics platform (2018 – 2019) First docs hire. Built the knowledge base from scratch using MkDocs. Wrote SDK quickstarts for Python, Node.js, and Ruby.

docs.text: Area Tools Doc platforms MkDocs / Material, Docusaurus, Confluence, Notion API documentation OpenAPI / Swagger, Postman, Redoc Writing & editing Markdown, MDX, AsciiDoc Version control Git, GitHub, GitHub Actions Scripting Python, Bash Methodologies Diátaxis, docs-as-code, agile/sprint-based delivery

docs.title: Simcha Lazarus
docs.text:
I’m a technical writer who works best where products are evolving quickly and documentation has to keep up. My experience includes merchant-facing guides, REST API documentation, release notes, internal knowledge content, and UX microcopy. I’m comfortable working closely with product managers, engineers, and support teams to turn moving parts into documentation that is clear, useful, and maintainable.

Email Me LinkedIn

docs.title: Technical Writer
docs.text:
Multiple SaaS Companies · 2024–Present

  • Create and maintain internal and external product documentation, including user guides, online help, REST API references, SDK documentation, and release notes.
  • Work with product managers and engineers to turn product features into clear customer-facing documentation.
  • Write and review UX microcopy and in-product text to improve clarity, consistency, and usability.
  • Test product features and APIs to validate documentation and keep content aligned with real user behavior.
  • Support releases by researching, editing, and delivering content under tight deadlines.
  • Maintain consistency and quality while managing multiple projects in fast-changing environments.

docs.title: Technical Writer
docs.text:
Global-e · 2023–2024

  • Collaborated with product managers to gather information on new features and translate them into clear merchant-facing documentation.
  • Wrote user guides covering financial flows, API usage, storefront features, price list uploads, and Global-e Shopify and GEM tool integrations.
  • Audited and updated the existing knowledge base in Confluence and Paligo to improve structure, clarity, and usability.
  • Migrated and maintained documentation in Paligo to keep content structured and easier to update across outputs.

  • Wrote a monthly internal company newsletter to strengthen internal communication.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
These are projects I’ve been working on in my own time, mostly around figuring out how to reuse old devices or get more out of them.

I usually start by trying something out, run into a few issues, and then write everything down properly so it’s clear and repeatable. The guides here reflect that process—step by step, including the parts that aren’t always obvious.

  • Rooting an Android Phone: A step-by-step guide to rooting an Android phone.
  • Turning an Old Android Phone into a Kitchen Kiosk: How I repurposed an Android phone into a simple digital kiosk.

docs.title: Turning an Android Phone into a Kitchen Kiosk
Overview
docs.text:
I had a drawer full of old devices that I could never quite bring myself to throw away. One of them was an older Xiaomi phone that had already become a small project in itself. I rooted it first so I could remove unnecessary apps, clean up the system, and get more control over how it behaved.

I cover that process separately in How to Root an Android Phone.

Once that was done, I decided to take it a step further and turn the phone into a kitchen kiosk—a simple, shared device with a few large buttons for everyday tasks.

My original goal was to build a full smart display, but I had to scale it back when I couldn’t get the phone to connect to an external screen. So I shifted to something simpler and more practical:

  • A clean home screen
  • Four large buttons for specific tasks
  • No distractions
  • No accidental taps into random apps
  • A setup simple enough for anyone in the house to use

This guide walks through the exact steps I followed.

docs.title: What I Wanted the Device to Do
docs.text:
Before changing anything, I decided exactly what the phone needed to do. I kept the scope narrow on purpose.

The home screen would include four buttons:

  1. Shopping List Open a list quickly and (ideally) allow adding items by voice.

  2. School Schedule Show what books my son needs each day—something that currently takes too much time every morning.

  3. Shabbat Times Display the week’s Shabbat times and eventually trigger music 15 minutes before it starts.

  4. Photos Open a slideshow.

docs.title: Step 1: Root and Clean the Phone
docs.text:
The first step was rooting the phone so I could remove manufacturer apps and reduce background activity.

I describe that process in detail in How to Root an Android Phone.

Once rooted, I removed apps I didn’t need. The goal wasn’t to strip the system completely—it was to keep only what supported the dashboard.

docs.title: What I removed
docs.text:
• Xiaomi bloatware
  • Unused Google apps
  • Preinstalled apps I wouldn’t use
  • Extra utilities that added clutter

docs.title: What I kept
docs.text:
• Core Android system apps
  • Google Play Services
  • Play Store
  • MacroDroid
  • A launcher
  • Apps needed for the dashboard features

docs.title: Why this mattered
docs.text: This step made the phone feel faster and simpler. More importantly, it helped shift the device from “old phone” to “dedicated tool.”

docs.title: Step 2: Choose a Home Screen Setup
docs.text: I tested a few launcher options before settling on one that fit the project.

docs.title: What I tried
docs.text:
• MIUI Launcher — Too restrictive and still felt like a regular phone
  • Niagara Launcher — Clean, but too list-based for shared use
  • Lawnchair — Flexible, but still felt like a standard Android setup
  • Square Home — Best fit for a tile-based dashboard

docs.title: What I chose
docs.text: I chose Square Home because it made it easy to create a simple, large-button interface.

docs.title: How I installed it
docs.text:
1. Downloaded it from the Play Store
  2. Opened Settings → Home Screen
  3. Set Square Home as the default launcher

After that, the phone booted directly into the new layout instead of the standard Xiaomi home screen.

docs.title: Step 3: Build the Four-Button Layout
docs.text: With Square Home set, I built the main interface.

docs.title: Goal
docs.text:
A home screen with only four large tiles:

  • Shopping List
  • School Schedule
  • Shabbat Times
  • Photos

docs.title: Setup process
docs.text:
1. Cleared everything from the home screen
  2. Added tiles one by one:
  3. Tap +
  4. Choose: Application / Shortcut / Launcher Action / Widget
  5. Resized each tile to make it easy to tap
  6. Labeled each tile clearly
  7. Locked the screen to landscape: Home Options → Behavior and UI → Screen Orientation → Landscape

docs.title: Step 4: Add Automation
docs.text:
Some behavior needed automation, so I installed MacroDroid.

I used it to:

  • Launch the dashboard on boot
  • Keep behavior consistent
  • Prepare for time-based actions

docs.title: Boot automation
docs.text: I wanted the dashboard to appear automatically after a restart.

docs.title: Setup
docs.text:
1. Open MacroDroid
  2. Create a new macro
  3. Set Trigger → Device Boot
  4. Set Action → Launch Application
  5. Select Square Home
  6. Save and test

Now the phone restarts directly into the dashboard instead of a standard Android screen.

docs.title: Current Status
docs.text:
At this point, the phone is close to how I originally pictured it. The structure is in place, but I still need to:

  • Connect each tile to its final function
  • Add some visual polish
  • Finish automation (like Shabbat reminders)

This is still a work in progress, and I’ll update this guide as I continue improving the setup.

docs.title: How to Root an Android Phone
docs.text:
Like many people, I have a drawer full of old devices that are no longer worth selling but still feel too useful to throw away. I had been looking for ways to repurpose some of that unused hardware, and one weekend I finally decided to start experimenting.

My first test project was an older Xiaomi phone. I had several ideas for it, but before turning it into something new, I wanted to clean it up, reclaim storage space, and gain more control over how it behaved.

That meant rooting it.

Rooting gives you administrative access to Android and allows deeper control over the operating system. With root access, you can remove unnecessary apps, reduce background activity, automate startup behavior, and customize the device in ways normally restricted by the manufacturer.

This was my first time rooting a phone. Below is the process I followed, including how rooting works, how to check whether a device is compatible, the risks involved, and how to complete the setup safely.

docs.title: What Rooting Means
docs.text:
Android is built on Linux. By default, phone manufacturers restrict access to core system areas. Rooting removes those restrictions and grants elevated permissions, commonly referred to as root access.

With root access, you can:

  • Remove preinstalled system apps
  • Change startup behavior
  • Run advanced automation tools
  • Improve performance on older hardware
  • Install specialized utilities
  • Repurpose devices for dedicated tasks

In my case, rooting let me strip the phone down to only the apps I needed for the project and noticeably improved battery life.

docs.title: Risks of Rooting Your Phone
docs.text:
For my first attempt, I intentionally used a spare device rather than a phone I depended on every day. Rooting can be useful, but it comes with real trade-offs.

  • Security risks: Apps granted root access can make deep system changes. That is helpful when you trust the app, but risky if you install something questionable.
  • Stability problems: Android components often depend on each other. Removing or changing the wrong package can cause crashes, battery drain, broken features, or boot loops.
  • Update issues: Rooted phones do not always handle normal software updates cleanly. Updates may fail, remove root access, or require manual reinstallation.
  • App compatibility: Some banking, payment, streaming, or workplace security apps may refuse to run on rooted devices.
  • Data loss: Unlocking the bootloader often wipes the phone completely. Back up your data first.
  • More maintenance: A rooted phone usually needs more hands-on management than a stock device.

docs.title: Before You Begin
docs.text:
Complete the following preparation steps before making any system changes.

  1. Check device compatibility Search online for your exact phone model and confirm that the bootloader can be unlocked. Devices from Google Pixel, Xiaomi, and OnePlus are generally easier to root than carrier-locked devices.

  2. Back up your data Save photos, contacts, files, notes, and anything else you want to keep.

  3. Download the required tools

  4. Android Platform Tools (ADB and Fastboot)

  5. Magisk (latest release)
  6. Device USB drivers (if required)

  7. Stock firmware for your exact device model

  8. Enable Developer Options

On the phone:

  • Open Settings > About Phone
  • Tap Build Number seven times
  • Open Developer Options
  • Enable USB Debugging
  • Enable OEM Unlocking

docs.title: Root the Device
docs.text: The exact process varies by manufacturer, but most modern Android devices follow the same general workflow.

docs.title: Step 1: Verify ADB Access
docs.text:
Connect the phone to your computer with a USB cable.

Run:

adb devices

Approve the USB debugging prompt on the phone.

Your device should appear with a status of device.

docs.title: Step 2: Reboot to Bootloader
docs.text:
Run:

adb reboot bootloader

The phone should restart into Fastboot or bootloader mode.

docs.title: Step 3: Unlock the Device
docs.text:
Run one of the following commands:

fastboot flashing unlock

or

fastboot oem unlock

Use the volume and power buttons on the phone to confirm.

Warning

Unlocking the bootloader usually performs a factory reset.

Once the phone reboots, repeat the Developer Options steps to re-enable USB Debugging if needed.

docs.title: Step 4: Obtain the Correct Firmware
docs.text:
Download the stock firmware that exactly matches the Android version currently installed on the phone.

Extract the package and locate:

boot.img

Warning

Using a boot image from the wrong firmware version can prevent the phone from booting.

docs.title: Step 5: Patch the Boot Image with Magisk
docs.text:
1. Install the Magisk APK on the phone.
  2. Copy boot.img to the device.
  3. Open Magisk.
  4. Tap Install.
  5. Select Patch a File.
  6. Choose boot.img.

Magisk creates a patched image file, usually named something similar to:

magisk_patched.img

Copy the patched image back to your computer.

docs.title: Step 6: Flash Root Access
docs.text:
Reboot into Fastboot mode if needed:

adb reboot bootloader

Then flash the patched image:

fastboot flash boot magisk_patched.img

Reboot:

fastboot reboot

The first boot may take longer than normal.

docs.title: Step 7: Confirm Success
docs.text:
Once Android loads:

  1. Open Magisk
  2. Confirm that Magisk shows as installed
  3. Grant root access only to apps you trust

You can also install a root checker app for independent confirmation.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text: Once rooted, you can begin tailoring the device to your needs.

docs.title: Remove Bloatware
docs.text: Uninstall or disable system apps you do not need.

docs.title: Improve Performance
docs.text: Reduce unnecessary background processes and startup tasks.

docs.title: Build a Dedicated Device
docs.text:
Use the phone as:

  • A wall dashboard
  • Smart home panel
  • Music player
  • Kitchen organizer
  • Family calendar
  • Media controller

docs.title: Manage Root Permissions
docs.text: Review root access regularly inside Magisk and revoke anything unnecessary.

docs.title: Device Bootloops After Flashing
docs.text:
The patched boot image likely did not match the installed firmware version.

Flash the original boot image:

fastboot flash boot boot.img
fastboot reboot

Then repeat the patching process with the correct firmware version.

docs.title: ADB Shows unauthorized
docs.text:
Unlock the phone screen and approve the USB debugging prompt.

If needed:

  • Disable USB Debugging
  • Re-enable it
  • Reconnect the cable

docs.title: OEM Unlocking Is Greyed Out
docs.text: Some manufacturers or carriers restrict bootloader unlocking. Check whether the device requires an official unlock request process.

docs.title: Rooted Apps Are Detected
docs.text: Enable Magisk features such as Zygisk and configure the deny list for affected apps.

docs.title: Final Thoughts
docs.text:
Rooting is not something to rush, but it can be a practical way to extend the life of older hardware.

For this project, it was the first step in turning an unused phone into something genuinely useful instead of leaving it in a drawer. With some care and patience, a retired device can become a focused tool again.

docs.title: How I Use AI in Technical Writing
docs.text:
Artificial intelligence has become a practical part of my technical writing workflow. I use tools like ChatGPT, Claude, and Copilot to improve speed, quality, and consistency, while keeping human judgment at the center of the process.

I don’t treat AI as a replacement for technical writing. Strong documentation still depends on understanding the product, knowing the audience, structuring information clearly, and making sure everything is accurate. What AI does well is remove friction from the writing process so I can focus more on creating content that actually helps users.

docs.title: Where AI Fits Into My Workflow
Research and Information Gathering
docs.text:
When I’m learning a new product, feature, or technical concept, AI helps me get up to speed quickly. It’s useful for explaining unfamiliar terms, summarizing topics, comparing approaches, or helping me understand how a system works before I start writing.

This is especially helpful when I’m working in a new domain or when access to subject matter experts is limited. It gives me a solid starting point so I can ask better questions and validate details more efficiently.

docs.title: Starting the First Draft
docs.text:
Blank pages slow everything down. I use AI to generate a first-pass outline, suggest structure, or turn rough notes into something more readable.

For user guides, that might include:

  • Step-by-step procedures
  • Onboarding guides
  • FAQ drafts
  • Troubleshooting structures
  • Release note summaries

That said, the draft is never final. I rewrite it, verify it against the product, and shape it based on what users actually need.

docs.title: Improving Existing Documentation
docs.text:
AI is just as useful once content already exists. I use it to:

  • Tighten unclear wording
  • Improve flow and readability
  • Simplify overly technical language
  • Adjust tone for different audiences
  • Remove repetition
  • Strengthen headings and structure

In practice, good documentation comes from strong editing. AI just helps me move through that process faster.

docs.title: Review and Error Detection
docs.text:
Before publishing, I use AI as an extra review layer. It helps catch:

  • Grammar issues
  • Inconsistent terminology
  • Missing steps
  • Ambiguous instructions
  • Repetitive phrasing
  • Formatting inconsistencies

It’s not a replacement for review—but it’s a useful second set of eyes.

docs.title: Writing Code and Technical Assets
docs.text:
Technical writers don’t just write anymore—we’re expected to work with code too. I use AI to help with:

  • Markdown and HTML formatting
  • CSS tweaks for documentation sites
  • YAML configuration
  • API examples
  • Small scripts and automation
  • Regex issues
  • Debugging documentation tooling

This saves time, especially in docs-as-code environments where you're constantly switching between writing and implementation.

docs.title: What I Learned Evaluating AI Tools
docs.text:
One of my most valuable early experiences with AI came during a short-term contract where the technical writing team was asked to evaluate ChatGPT and Claude.

We tested how each tool responded to different prompts and documented the results to improve how we used AI in our workflows. That included comparing output quality, structure, tone, and accuracy across different types of writing tasks.

Two things became clear pretty quickly.

First, each tool behaves differently. One might be better at structure, another at tone, another at handling technical instructions. You can’t assume they’ll all give you the same kind of output.

Second, prompt quality makes a huge difference. The more specific and structured the prompt, the better the result. Vague prompts led to vague content. Clear prompts led to usable drafts.

That experience changed how I work with AI. It’s not about asking it to “write something”—it’s about guiding it properly so the output is actually useful.

docs.title: My Approach to Using AI Responsibly
docs.text:
I use AI as a tool, not a shortcut. That means:

  • I verify technical accuracy
  • I don’t include sensitive or confidential information
  • I edit everything for clarity and tone
  • I make sure content reflects how the product actually works
  • I keep the user’s needs front and center

AI can generate content quickly. My job is to make sure that content is correct, clear, and genuinely helpful.

docs.title: Final Thoughts
docs.text:
AI is now a regular part of how I work. It helps me research faster, draft more efficiently, edit more effectively, and solve technical problems along the way.

But the value isn’t in the tool—it’s in how you use it. Knowing how to prompt well, when to trust the output, and when to step in and rewrite—that’s where the real skill comes in.

At the end of the day, good documentation still comes down to clear thinking and good judgment. AI just helps me get there faster.

docs.title: PageTurner API
docs.text:
Book Recommendation API — Developer Documentation

Version: 1.1.0 · Base URL: https://api.pageturner.io/v1 · Format: JSON

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The PageTurner API lets you build book recommendation features into any application. Clients submit structured reader preferences such as genre, mood, page count, minimum rating, and optional free-text context, and the API returns ranked book recommendations with metadata and match explanations.

Use cases include:

  • Reading apps with personalized recommendations
  • Library discovery tools
  • E-commerce product discovery
  • Chatbot and voice-assistant reading features

docs.title: Authentication
docs.text:
All requests must include a valid API key as a Bearer token in the Authorization header.

Authorization: Bearer YOUR_API_KEY

API keys are available from the PageTurner Developer Portal.

docs.title: Access tiers
docs.text: Tier Rate limit Intended use Free 100 requests / day Personal projects and prototyping Reader 5,000 requests / day Small production applications Librarian Unlimited Enterprise and high-volume integrations

docs.title: Authentication errors
docs.text:
If the API key is missing, invalid, or expired, the API returns:

{
  "error": {
    "code": "UNAUTHORIZED",
    "message": "Missing or invalid API key.",
    "status": 401
  }
}

docs.title: Content type
docs.text: All request bodies must be sent as application/json.

docs.title: Success envelope
docs.text:
Successful responses use the following structure:

{
  "data": [],
  "meta": {
    "total_results": 4,
    "limit": 4,
    "offset": 0,
    "has_more": false,
    "query_time_ms": 287,
    "request_id": "req_01JQ8J8Y7J6R7YFQZX4M3M9H2A"
  }
}

docs.title: Error envelope
docs.text:
Error responses use the following structure:

{
  "error": {
    "code": "INVALID_PARAMS",
    "message": "Validation failed.",
    "status": 422,
    "details": [
      {
        "field": "min_rating",
        "issue": "Must be a number between 0 and 5."
      }
    ],
    "request_id": "req_01JQ8J8Y7J6R7YFQZX4M3M9H2A"
  }
}

docs.title: Request IDs
docs.text: Every response includes a request_id value. Log this value when contacting support.

docs.title: Get recommendations
docs.text:
POST /recommendations

Returns ranked book recommendations based on one or more filter fields.

Full URL: https://api.pageturner.io/v1/recommendations

At least one filter field is required.

docs.title: Request body
docs.text: Field Type Required Description genres array of strings No Genre filters. Accepted values are listed in GET /genres. moods array of strings No Mood filters. Accepted values are listed in GET /moods. min_pages integer No Minimum page count. Must be >= 1. max_pages integer No Maximum page count. Must be >= min_pages. min_rating number No Minimum Goodreads rating. Must be between 0 and 5. Example: 4.0. query string No Free-text reading intent or comparison prompt. Maximum 500 characters. limit integer No Number of recommendations to return. Range: 1-10. Default: 4. offset integer No Number of results to skip for pagination. Minimum: 0. Default: 0. sort string No Result ordering. Accepted values: relevance, rating, publication_year. Default: relevance.

docs.title: Validation rules
docs.text:
• At least one of genres, moods, min_pages, max_pages, min_rating, or query must be present.
  • Empty arrays are not allowed.
  • max_pages must be greater than or equal to min_pages.
  • query must not exceed 500 characters.
  • Unsupported enum values return 422 INVALID_PARAMS.

docs.title: Example request
docs.text:
POST /v1/recommendations
Authorization: Bearer YOUR_API_KEY
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "genres": ["historical-fiction", "literary-fiction"],
  "moods": ["slow-burn", "emotional"],
  "min_pages": 400,
  "max_pages": 600,
  "min_rating": 4.0,
  "query": "Something like Donna Tartt but set outside the US, preferably Japan or Europe.",
  "limit": 4,
  "offset": 0,
  "sort": "relevance"
}

docs.title: Response fields
docs.text: Field Type Description id string Stable PageTurner book identifier title string Book title author string Author full name publication_year integer Year of first publication page_count integer Estimated page count goodreads_rating number Average Goodreads rating goodreads_url string Goodreads URL genres array of strings Canonical genre values moods array of strings Canonical mood values summary string Short editorial summary why_matched string Explanation of why the title matched the request

docs.title: Example response
docs.text:
{
  "data": [
    {
      "id": "book_7wYk29fM",
      "title": "The Master and Margarita",
      "author": "Mikhail Bulgakov",
      "publication_year": 1967,
      "page_count": 384,
      "goodreads_rating": 4.32,
      "goodreads_url": "https://www.goodreads.com/book/show/117833",
      "genres": ["literary-fiction", "historical-fiction", "classic"],
      "moods": ["dark-atmospheric", "thought-provoking"],
      "summary": "A satirical novel set in Soviet Moscow, where the Devil arrives and disrupts literary, political, and spiritual life.",
      "why_matched": "Matched for literary tone, European setting, strong critical reputation, and a rating above 4.0."
    }
  ],
  "meta": {
    "total_results": 14,
    "limit": 4,
    "offset": 0,
    "has_more": true,
    "query_time_ms": 287,
    "request_id": "req_01JQ8J8Y7J6R7YFQZX4M3M9H2A"
  }
}

docs.title: Empty result behavior
docs.text:
If the request is valid but no books match the filters, the API returns 200 OK with an empty data array.

{
  "data": [],
  "meta": {
    "total_results": 0,
    "limit": 4,
    "offset": 0,
    "has_more": false,
    "query_time_ms": 109,
    "request_id": "req_01JQ8JFBJZXQ3WQFGH4W2B7Y2F"
  }
}

docs.title: List supported genres
docs.text:
GET /genres

Returns the full list of supported genre values for request validation and UI dropdowns.

docs.title: Example response
docs.text:
{
  "data": {
    "genres": [
      "literary-fiction",
      "science-fiction",
      "fantasy",
      "mystery-thriller",
      "historical-fiction",
      "romance",
      "non-fiction",
      "horror",
      "biography",
      "classic"
    ]
  },
  "meta": {
    "request_id": "req_01JQ8JH4D3VGX2P6M1W0Z5K4Q1"
  }
}

docs.title: List supported moods
docs.text:
GET /moods

Returns the full list of supported mood values for request validation and UI dropdowns.

docs.title: Example response
docs.text:
{
  "data": {
    "moods": [
      "feel-good",
      "dark-atmospheric",
      "fast-paced",
      "slow-burn",
      "thought-provoking",
      "witty",
      "emotional"
    ]
  },
  "meta": {
    "request_id": "req_01JQ8JHXC7F6H1D1T9XQ3R0Y5V"
  }
}

docs.title: Error Codes
docs.text: Code Status Description UNAUTHORIZED 401 Missing, invalid, or expired API key FORBIDDEN 403 Your plan does not permit the request INVALID_PARAMS 422 Request body failed validation RATE_LIMITED 429 You exceeded your request quota SERVER_ERROR 500 Internal error

docs.title: Validation error example
docs.text:
{
  "error": {
    "code": "INVALID_PARAMS",
    "message": "Validation failed.",
    "status": 422,
    "details": [
      {
        "field": "max_pages",
        "issue": "Must be greater than or equal to min_pages."
      },
      {
        "field": "genres[0]",
        "issue": "Unsupported genre value 'Historical Fiction'. Use canonical slug values from GET /genres."
      }
    ],
    "request_id": "req_01JQ8JKERQ5YV9DRQJ28KJ3B8N"
  }
}

docs.title: Rate Limits
docs.text:
Rate limits are enforced per API key and plan.

When a rate limit is exceeded, the API returns 429 Too Many Requests.

{
  "error": {
    "code": "RATE_LIMITED",
    "message": "You have exceeded your plan's request quota.",
    "status": 429,
    "request_id": "req_01JQ8JM2FQ1N1K7S5A2A6R1D4M"
  }
}

docs.title: Sorting and Ranking
docs.text:
The sort field controls result ordering:

Value Description relevance Best semantic and filter match to the request rating Highest Goodreads rating first publication_year Most recently published titles first

If sort is omitted, results are returned in relevance order.

docs.title: Data Source Notes
docs.text:
Recommendation metadata is sourced from Goodreads and PageTurner editorial processing.

Clients should not assume Goodreads values are real-time. Ratings, URLs, and metadata may be cached and refreshed periodically.

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
The example below fetches four fast-paced fantasy recommendations with a minimum rating of 4.0.

curl -X POST https://api.pageturner.io/v1/recommendations \
  -H 'Authorization: Bearer YOUR_API_KEY' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{
    "genres": ["fantasy"],
    "moods": ["fast-paced"],
    "min_rating": 4.0,
    "limit": 4,
    "sort": "relevance"
  }'

docs.title: Integration Notes
docs.text:
For production clients:

  • cache /genres and /moods
  • log request_id values for debugging
  • handle 429 and 5xx responses with retry logic
  • treat empty recommendation lists as valid successful responses
  • use canonical slug values from lookup endpoints rather than hard-coding display labels

docs.title: Writing Samples
docs.text:
This section includes a mix of writing samples representing the different types of documentation I’ve worked on—user guides, API docs, technical articles, and a few project write-ups. It gives a good sense of how I explain things, structure content, and adjust my writing depending on the audience, whether that’s end users or more technical readers.

  • AI in Technical Writing: My perspective on using AI in my work as a technical writer.
  • PageTurn API: Documentation for a fictional API inspired by one of my favorite activities—reading and collecting books.
  • Sample User Guide: A PDF user guide I wrote for a client, explaining how to use one of their products.
  • Release Notes: Sample release notes for the fictional PageTurn API.

docs.title: Version 1.2.0
docs.text: Release Date: April 22, 2026

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: This release introduces new filtering capabilities, performance improvements, and updates to request validation. It also includes breaking changes to parameter naming and pagination.

docs.title: New Capabilities
docs.text:
• Mood-based filtering via mood parameter
  • Author-based recommendations via similar_to_author
  • Expanded genre support:
  • Historical Fiction
  • Memoir
  • True Crime
  • Short Stories

docs.title: Enhancements
docs.text:
• Improved response times (approximately 25% reduction in latency)
  • Updated ranking algorithm for improved recommendation accuracy
  • Standardized error response schema across endpoints

docs.title: Breaking Changes
docs.text: Change Description Action Required Parameter rename feeling → mood Update all requests to use mood Pagination update Page-based → Cursor-based Update integration logic to support cursors

docs.title: Bug Fixes
docs.text:
• Rating filters are now consistently applied
  • Duplicate recommendation issue resolved
  • External link validation corrected

docs.title: Security Updates
docs.text:
• Strengthened request validation
  • Updated rate limiting thresholds
  • Improved handling of expired API keys

docs.title: Deprecation Notice
docs.text:
The following features are deprecated and will be removed in a future release:

  • feeling parameter (use mood instead)
  • Page-based pagination

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: This release focuses on improving recommendation quality and ensuring consistency across endpoints. Clients using deprecated fields should update their integrations ahead of future releases.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: One of my recent roles involved writing user guides for a company that created monetization tools for mobile game publishers. Below is a document that I wrote describing how to navigate the dashboard to to create and edit content.

docs.title: Context
docs.text: Product type: Gaming / SaaS platform Audience: Content managers and non-technical users Documentation type: User guide / feature documentation
